1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

40	
41	F = t.TypeVar("F", bound=t.Callable[..., t.Any])
42	K = t.TypeVar("K")
43	V = t.TypeVar("V")
44	
45	
46	def ignore_case(value: V) -> V:
47	    """For use as a postprocessor for :func:`make_attrgetter`. Converts strings
48	    to lowercase and returns other types as-is."""
49	    if isinstance(value, str):
50	        return t.cast(V, value.lower())
51	
52	    return value
53	
54	
55	def make_attrgetter(
56	    environment: "Environment",
57	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
58	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
59	    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
60	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]:
61	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a
62	    passed object with the rules of the environment.  Dots are allowed
63	    to access attributes of attributes.  Integer parts in paths are
64	    looked up as integers.
65	    """
66	    parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute)
67	
68	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.Any:
69	        for part in parts:
70	            item = environment.getitem(item, part)
71	
72	            if default is not None and isinstance(item, Undefined):
73	                item = default
74	
75	        if postprocess is not None:
76	            item = postprocess(item)
77	
78	        return item
79	
80	    return attrgetter
81	
82	
83	def make_multi_attrgetter(
84	    environment: "Environment",
85	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
86	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
87	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.List[t.Any]]:
88	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given comma separated
89	    attributes from a passed object with the rules of the environment.
90	    Dots are allowed to access attributes of each attribute.  Integer
91	    parts in paths are looked up as integers.
92	
93	    The value returned by the returned callable is a list of extracted
94	    attribute values.
95	
96	    Examples of attribute: "attr1,attr2", "attr1.inner1.0,attr2.inner2.0", etc.
97	    """
98	    if isinstance(attribute, str):
99	        split: t.Sequence[t.Union[str, int, None]] = attribute.split(",")
100	    else:
101	        split = [attribute]
102	
103	    parts = [_prepare_attribute_parts(item) for item in split]
104	
105	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.List[t.Any]:
106	        items = [None] * len(parts)
107	
108	        for i, attribute_part in enumerate(parts):
109	            item_i = item
110	
111	            for part in attribute_part:
112	                item_i = environment.getitem(item_i, part)
113	
114	            if postprocess is not None:
115	                item_i = postprocess(item_i)
116	
117	            items[i] = item_i
118	
119	        return items
120	
121	    return attrgetter
122	
123	
124	def _prepare_attribute_parts(
125	    attr: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]]
126	) -> t.List[t.Union[str, int]]:
127	    if attr is None:
128	        return []
129	
130	    if isinstance(attr, str):
131	        return [int(x) if x.isdigit() else x for x in attr.split(".")]
132	
133	    return [attr]
134	
135	
136	def do_forceescape(value: "t.Union[str, HasHTML]") -> Markup:
137	    """Enforce HTML escaping.  This will probably double escape variables."""
138	    if hasattr(value, "__html__"):
139	        value = t.cast("HasHTML", value).__html__()

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" group will have two values. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive) return out def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], case_sensitive: bool, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": sort_key = expr if case_sensitive else (lambda item: ignore_case(expr(item))) groups = [] for _, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_key), sort_key): values = list(values) groups.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) return groups

content.text: Now let's verify the file compiles and test behavior quickly.

input.command:
python -c "
import jinja2
env = jinja2.Environment()
t = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city':'CA'},{'city':'NY'},{'city':'ca'}]
print(t.render(users=users))
t2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(t2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Works as expected. Let's run the existing test suite for filters to ensure nothing broke.

content.text:
The patch is written to `patch.txt`. Summary:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- Factored the grouping logic into a shared `_group_by` helper: it sorts/groups using a case-insensitive key by default (via `ignore_case`) but reports each group's `grouper` using the original-case value from the first item in that group — matching the described behavior (e.g., `"CA"`/`"ca"` group together, output key keeps first-seen casing).
- Updated docstring with explanation and a `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually and via `tests/test_filters.py` and `tests/test_async_filters.py` (all passing, 122 + 41 tests).

Note: agent2 is also editing `do_groupby`/`sync_do_groupby` in the same file (adding a `with_counts` param) — I sent them a coordination message describing my exact changes so they can build on top of the new `_group_by` helper rather than clobbering it.

result:
The patch is written to `patch.txt`. Summary:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- Factored the grouping logic into a shared `_group_by` helper: it sorts/groups using a case-insensitive key by default (via `ignore_case`) but reports each group's `grouper` using the original-case value from the first item in that group — matching the described behavior (e.g., `"CA"`/`"ca"` group together, output key keeps first-seen casing).
- Updated docstring with explanation and a `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually and via `tests/test_filters.py` and `tests/test_async_filters.py` (all passing, 122 + 41 tests).

Note: agent2 is also editing `do_groupby`/`sync_do_groupby` in the same file (adding a `with_counts` param) — I sent them a coordination message describing my exact changes so they can build on top of the new `_group_by` helper rather than clobbering it.
